back: Learning from Las Vegas (1972) (Denise Scott Brown)

front: Architect: co-authored Learning from Las Vegas (1972) with Robert Venturi and Steven Izenour; grew out of a Yale studio studying A&P parking lots
Architect: Learning from Las Vegas introduces 'duck' (building whose form represents its function, named for Long Island's Big Duck) vs. 'decorated shed' (functional building using vivid signage)
back: Learning from Las Vegas (1972) (Denise Scott Brown)

front: Architect: publication of Learning from Las Vegas led to the 'Whites vs. Grays' debate — Grays championed postmodern ornamentation; Whites (Eisenman, Meier) maintained pure modernism; names popularized by Robert Stern
back: Learning from Las Vegas (1972) (Denise Scott Brown)

front: Architect: Michael Graves defected from the Whites to the Grays; Philip Johnson's defection was highest-profile, marked by his AT&T Building's ornamental top
back: Learning from Las Vegas (1972) (Denise Scott Brown)

front: Architect: Learning from Las Vegas uses the La Tourette Monastery as an example and traces its influences on Neiman Marcus and Levittowns; contains a section called 'Space as God'
back: Learning from Las Vegas (1972) (Denise Scott Brown)

front: Architect: Aron Vinegar's 'I am a Monument' is a critical examination of Learning from Las Vegas
back: Learning from Las Vegas (1972) (Denise Scott Brown)

front: Architect: boycotted the 1991 Pritzker Prize ceremony when her husband Robert Venturi alone was awarded the prize; she never received it
work: 'Room at the Top?' / Pritzker Prize controversy

front: Architect: essay 'Room at the Top? Sexism and the Star System in Architecture' critiques how 'starchitect' culture obscures female collaborators' contributions
work: 'Room at the Top?' / Pritzker Prize controversy

front: Architect: 'Room at the Top?' cites Budd Schulberg's idea of 'Star Quality' and compares Venturi to Howard Roark in the context of the star system
work: 'Room at the Top?' / Pritzker Prize controversy

back: Buildings and Campus Plans (Denise Scott Brown)

front: Building: collaborated with Venturi to design the Sainsbury Wing of the National Gallery in London
Building: collaborated with Venturi to design the Seattle Art Museum
Architect: designed campus plans for Williams College, Bryn Mawr College, and the University of Michigan
Architect: revived Frank Furness's reputation after studying in Philadelphia under Louis Kahn
